creator: jamie@catflap.org
text:
> --- Comment #2 from Bryan Drewery <bdrewery@FreeBSD.org> ---
>
> Why change? This would just cause confusion for users and cause them to lose
> their screen.
>
> You can also put this into a Makefile.local or your make.conf for when using
> the sysutils/screen dir.

Fair enough.

I've already gone down the '.if ${.CURDIR}=="/usr/ports/sysutils/screen"' ...
route in make.conf, but thought it may be useful - I didn't realise that
reinstalling screen with sessions still active was such a common thing
until I read the various recent PRs / commits today. I'd personally never
upgrade it whilst it's in use!

Why change?

I'm personally not comfortable having a piece of software reliant on being
able to create a directory/file of known name in a world-writable parent
directory. At best it seems untidy.
I feel that the parent used should be user-writable only, and either owned
by the user running the program or root (in the case of setuid->0 scripts)

But maybe that's just me. No worries, but you did ask why! :-)

As it is, a user could deny others the use of screen, by creating /tmp/screens
themselves (yes, to get around that, a user could copy the binary or recompile
and use a local screen directory, but that's not really a valid argument)

Still, as long as no race-condition exists, I'm worrying about nothing.
It's hardly a likely situation to occur, and it's not exactly life-threatening,
but it doesn't seem tidy to me!

It's probably just an OCD thing - I suppose the directory could be created
at boot-time, but then if the rm_tmp periodic script is being used, it
would need to go into ignore, because otherwise if no screens are active
it will get deleted.... And then, you basically have a permanent directory
in a parent designed for temporary things :-)

So I reasoned that without a /roottmp, then /var/run would be the next
best thing (though admittedly it isn't quite an exact fit as per hier(7))

> --- Comment #3 from Cy Schubert <cy@FreeBSD.org> ---
>
> Agreed. Also, the man page says,
>
> The  "socket  directory"  defaults either to $HOME/.screen or simply to
> /tmp/screens or preferably to  /usr/local/screens  chosen  at  compile-
> time. If screen is installed setuid-root, then the administrator should
> compile screen with an adequate (not NFS mounted) socket directory.  If
> screen  is  not  running setuid-root, the user can specify any mode 700
> directory in the environment variable $SCREENDIR.

Yep, 'preferably' /usr/local/screens rather than /tmp - but I thought
if going down that route, better to use a non-world-writeable directory
that already exists for the purposes of storing temporary files that
don't need to exist after a reboot... i.e. /var/run! :-)

> You can file off the setuid bit, which only affects multiuser mode, and
> set SCREENDIR.

Indeed, and I've been thinking of doing that anyway for other reasons
(though I assume it will no longer be able to manipulate utx either)

Still, if I was just considering myself, I'd make more use of
CONFIGURE_ARGS. EXTRA_PATCHES, and other such gubbings and not bother
raising PRs at all!

Anyway, as I said, no worries. Just throwing my thoughts out there.

Thanks for the replies, and apologies for 'bikeshedding', and the
unintentional 'spam' (that may sound bitchy, but I mean it sincerely)

Cheers, Jamie
